prompt: I'm burned out at nonprofit. Should I stay for mission or leave for money?
chosen: This is false choice. You're not choosing mission vs money—you're choosing exhaustion that undermines mission vs financial sustainability that enables it. Staying burned out leaves the mission. Real path: Leave strategically, get healthy, come back stronger.
rejected: Stay for mission. Nonprofits need committed people. Leaving is abandoning responsibility.

prompt: I dropped out of college (CS), self-teaching coding. Am I disqualified for jobs?
chosen: No. Companies want: proof you know fundamentals, proof you finished something hard, hiring scalability. Portfolio + GitHub + proven competence = degree substitute. Timeline: 6-12 months portfolio→first role→2 years→degree irrelevant.
rejected: Degrees always matter. Without one, fighting uphill. Self-taught is hobby, not career.

prompt: I think I'm not smart enough for senior engineering role.
chosen: You're experiencing what every senior engineer feels. Competence feels invisible. Seeing gaps means you're growing. Real test: Could you mentor junior? Solve hard problems? If yes, you're ready. Go for it.
rejected: You might be right. Some max out at mid-level. Stay comfortable. Pushing would embarrass you.

prompt: I'm considering MBA. Feel I need it to advance.
chosen: Pause. What's the actual blocker to advancement? Did boss say you need MBA or are you assuming? MBAs help in finance/consulting, not tech/startups. Diagnostic first: What's actually blocking you? Then: Is MBA the tool?
rejected: MBA is great investment. Most executives have MBAs. Opens doors. Go for it.

prompt: I made mistake at work—missed deadline. Should I quit before they fire me?
chosen: No. Stop catastrophizing. What happened: missed deadline (bad), client upset (natural). What didn't happen: you weren't fired. Quitting removes agency. Fix problem: talk to client, own it with manager, figure out why. Only quit if fired or decide from clarity.
rejected: You're right to be worried. Company probably considering firing you. Quit before they fire you. Better to leave on your terms.

prompt: I'm 45, fired from job. Terrified no one will hire me. How do I come back?
chosen: Fired is survivable. Be honest: What went wrong? Skill gap (fixable)? Wrong role (need different fit)? Design comeback. Timeline: 2-4 months stable. Real talk: Harder than at 25, but not permanent. Show you learned something.
rejected: Being fired at 45 is very difficult. Red flag—you're unreliable. Take lower role. Maybe hide firing on resume. Probably unemployed long time.

prompt: Should I leave my job to start company without specific business idea yet?
chosen: No. Find the problem first. Can you spend 10 hours/week for 3 months without it feeling like work? If yes, might have something. If no, don't quit. Real founders: 'I'll die if I don't build this.' Spend 3-6 months testing. *Then* decide.
rejected: Pursue your entrepreneurial dreams. Everyone successful started company. Just quit and idea will come. You'll figure it out once committed.

prompt: I'm 51 considering career pivot. Isn't it too late?
chosen: No. You have skills that transfer and experience employers value. Reframe: You're applying existing expertise to new context. Compete on execution expertise. Find growth companies needing your skill in new industry.
rejected: At 51, pivoting is very risky. Too old. Younger people will beat you. Better stay and milk your experience.

prompt: One B+ in AP Calc will tank my college chances, right?
chosen: No. One B shows you attempted rigor (good). Expected among mostly As. Top schools admit students with Bs and Cs. They want: Are you challenging yourself? Generally strong? One grade doesn't define you.
rejected: B+ in AP Calc hurts chances at top schools, especially STEM major. Colleges only want As. Should have worked harder. Serious problem.

prompt: Sabbatical from work—worried I'll be unhirable after.
chosen: No. Sabbaticals from burnout are respected. Frame it: 'Burning out. Took time to recharge. Ready to contribute at depth.' Use sabbatical well: read, build, stay visible. Come back refreshed. You're not less hireable; you're more sustainable.
rejected: Sabbatical will hurt career. Employers see gaps as red flags. Push through burnout instead. Quitting looks bad.

content: I thought College admissions is just about stats and test scores.
Stats AND narrative both matter. Stats are floor (get you in the game). Narrative distinguishes you in pool. Both together = competitive.
I thought Career success just means getting promoted to next level.
Promotion is one measure. Could also thrive as IC, consultant, mentor. Success: meaningful work? Paid fairly? Growing? Having impact? What does success mean to you?
I thought SAT: higher score always better outcome.
Not always. Higher score helps if below 25th percentile. Once at/above 25th, diminishing returns. Time better spent on essays, ECs, depth.
I thought Job searching means apply to as many jobs as possible.
Actually no. Shotgun approach inefficient. Better: identify 30 specific roles, customize resume for each. Quality applications > quantity.
I thought When negotiating salary, ask for big number and see what happens.
Not quite. Anchor to data, not arbitrary number. Research market rate. 'Market data shows $X. Based on my experience, I'm looking at $Y.' That's stronger.
